coredata.dc:title: Construction of ratio fluorescence sensor based on CdTe quantum dots and benzocoumarin-3-carboxylic acid for Hg2+ detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: WANG, Kun
DONG, Er-Fei
FANG, Min
CHEN, Ting
ZHU, Wei-Ju
LI, Cun
coredata.dc:description: A new ratio fluorescence sensor based on mercaptamine capped cadmium telluride quantum dots (Mea-CdTe) and benzocoumarin-3-carboxylic acid (BTA) were constructed. The ratio fluorescence sensor (BTA@Mea-CdTe) was synthesized by amidation reaction between the amino groups on the surface of Mea-CdTe quantum dots and the carboxyl groups of BTA. It is found that Hg2+ ions react with the amino groups on the surface of the quantum dots in the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor to quench the fluorescence, but it has no effect on the fluorescence of grafted BTA. The sensor has good selectivity and high sensitivity for Hg2+ ions detection. The detection linear range is 0.4–1.2 µM, and the detection limit is 0.03 µM. This ratio fluorescence sensor can be used for Hg2+ visual recognition in water sample detection with obvious color change.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mea-CdTe quantum dots
Ratio fluorescence sensor
Benzocoumarin-3-carboxylic acid
Hg2+ ions detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and optical properties of BTA@Mea-CdTe
Detection of Hg2 + ions by BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor
Fluorescence response mechanism of BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor to Hg2+ ions

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The mechanism of interaction between Hg2+ ion and BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor.
(a) TEM image of Mea-CdTe quantum dots. (b) HRTEM image of Mea-CdTe quantum dots. (c) TEM image of BTA@ Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor. (d) HRTEM image of BTA@ Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of Mea-CdTe quantum dots and mercaptamine hydrochloride.
Infrared spectra of BTA, Mea-CdTe quantum dots and BTA@Mea-CdTe.
(a) UV-Vis absorption spectra of the BTA, bare Mea-CdTe and BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the BTA, bare Mea-CdTe and BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor.

(a) Fluorescence response of BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor to Hg2+ ions and other metal ions at a concentration of 10 µM. (b) Histogram of the fluorescence intensity of the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor at the emission peak of 436 nm and 575 nm after the addition of Hg2+ ions and other metal ions.
Ions interference of BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor.

(a) The fluorescence spectrum of different concentrations of Hg2+ (0 µM, 0.25 µM, 0.5 µM, 0.75 µM, 1 µM, 1.5 µM, 2 µM) added to 1×10−5 M Mea-CdTe quantum dots. (b) Linear relationship diagram between F 0−F and the concentration of Hg2+ added to 1×10−5 M Mea-CdTe quantum dots. (c) Fluorescence titration spectra of 1×10−5 M BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor in the presence of Hg2+ ions of different concentrations (0–1.4 µM). (d) The linear relationship between Hg2+ ion concentration (0.4–1.2 µM) and the fluorescence intensity ratio I 575/I 436.

Mea-CdTe quantum dots and BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor fluorescence color change with Hg2+ ions under 340 nm irradiation (Hg2+ concentration is 0 µM, 0.2 µM, 0.4 µM, 0.6 µM, 0.8 µM, 1.0 µM from left to right).
(a) Infrared spectra of BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor and BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor with Hg2+. (b) XPS (N1s) of BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor and BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor with Hg2+.
Comparison of different fluorescent sensors for Hg2+ detection.
Analytical results of the tap water and lake water samples.

head.ce:title: Construction of ratio fluorescence sensor based on CdTe quantum dots and benzocoumarin-3-carboxylic acid for Hg2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: AnHui Province Key Laboratory of Chemistry for Inorganic/Organic Hybrid Functionalized Materials, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: AnHui Province Key Laboratory of Chemistry for Inorganic/Organic Hybrid Functionalized Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Anhui University, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new ratio fluorescence sensor based on mercaptamine capped cadmium telluride quantum dots (Mea-CdTe) and benzocoumarin-3-carboxylic acid (BTA) were constructed. The ratio fluorescence sensor (BTA@Mea-CdTe) was synthesized by amidation reaction between the amino groups on the surface of Mea-CdTe quantum dots and the carboxyl groups of BTA. It is found that Hg2+ ions react with the amino groups on the surface of the quantum dots in the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor to quench the fluorescence, but it has no effect on the fluorescence of grafted BTA. The sensor has good selectivity and high sensitivity for Hg2+ ions detection. The detection linear range is 0.4–1.2 µM, and the detection limit is 0.03 µM. This ratio fluorescence sensor can be used for Hg2+ visual recognition in water sample detection with obvious color change.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is one of the most harmful heavy metal ions pollutant to environmental safety and human health. Compared with other common metal pollutants and some persistent organic pollutants, mercury pollution is more serious for its high toxicity. The accumulation of Hg2+ ions in human cells will cause permanent damage to the nervous system, leading to a series of diseases from serious damage to important organs [1–4]. A variety of analytical techniques can be used to detect Hg2+ ions, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5], electrochemical method [6], atomic absorption spectrometry [7], and high performance liquid chromatography [8]. However, these methods are complicated, costly, and time-consuming, and require a series of complex treatments on the samples before detection. Therefore, fluorescence analysis is widely used in biosensors and environmental detection [9–12] and other fields, which also includes the application of fluorescence quantum dots [13,14] for its high sensitivity and good specificity and convenient operation.

The detection method based on ratio fluorescence is currently a popular detection method based on the target reaction of the signal probe, which can minimize inference errors and environmental factors, and improve the selectivity and sensitivity of the detection system [15]. In recent years, the ratio fluorescence sensor has attracted great attention for the detection of ions and bio-organic molecules, because it can provide visual detection for the target analyte [16]. Meanwhile, quantum dot-based nanosensors have great application prospects in biology [17–19]. In the design of the ratio sensing system, one donor is responsible for the target specific binding with quenching or enhancing effect, and the other donor remains unchanged fluorescence intensity as reference to achieve visual detection [20]. Lu et al. designed a ratio fluorescence probe based on silicon quantum dots for specific identification of Cu2+ ions [21]. Zhu et al. designed a biosensor for simultaneous detection of mercury(II) and silver(I) ions [22]. Zhang et al. designed a ratio fluorescence probe based on CdSe quantum dots for specific recognition of Fe3+ [23]. However, there are few reports on the construction of organic probe functionalized quantum dots ratio fluorescence sensor to detect Hg2+ ions.

Considering the excellent optical properties of CdTe quantum dots used as sensor for ion detection, and the fluorescence of benzocoumarin-3 formic acid (BTA) is relatively strong, stable, and almost not interfered by metal ions, so it can be used as a reference signal to identify ions [24]. In this paper, mercaptamine capped CdTe quantum dots (Mea-CdTe) were synthesized in aqueous solution. Then, BTA was grafted onto the surface of Mea-CdTe quantum dots by EDC/NHS [25] activation to construct a new type ratio fluorescence sensor (BTA@Mea-CdTe). The new ratio fluorescence sensor is used for Hg2+ ions detection. The color of the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor changes more obviously compared with the recognition effect of pure Mea-CdTe quantum dots. The mechanism of interaction between Hg2+ ion and BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor is shown in Scheme 1 . The combination of Hg2+ ions with the amino groups of Mea-CdTe quantum dots quenches the fluorescence of the Mea-CdTe quantum dots.

Tellurium (Te), cadmium chloride (CdCl2•2.5H2O), mercaptamine hydrochloride (C2H8ClNS), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), 2-hydroxy-1-naphthaldehyde (C11H8O2), diethyl malonate (C7H12O4), hexahydropyridine (C5H11N), absolute ethanol (C2H5OH), glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, C4H5NO3), and carbodiimide (EDC, C8H11N3•HCl) were obtained from Aladdin reagent Co. Ltd. (China). The metal ions of Mg2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+ are all nitrate. The metal ions of Ni2+, Fe2+ are all chloride. Reagents used in this study were all analytically pure grade without further treatment. The ultrapure water was used throughout the whole experiments. The synthesis of BTA was described in the Supplementary data.

Ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (UV-1800), fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-2500), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (IR-prestige-21), high resolution transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi) .

Appropriate modifications were made according to the method reported in the literature of previously synthesized CdTe [26]. The deionized water is de-aerated through N2 for 1 h to remove the dissolved oxygen. 0.183 g (0.8 mmol) CdCl2•2.5H2O and 0.182 g (1.6 mmol) mercaptamine put into a three-necked flask filled with 100 mL pretreated DI water. The solution stirs under N2 atmosphere for 50 min. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 5.6 with 1 M NaOH solution. The NaHTe solution is prepared by addition of 0.051 g (0.4 mmol) Te and 0.045 g (1.2 mmol) NaBH4 in 40 mL H2O under stirring in N2 atmosphere for 4 h. The obtained colorless and clear NaHTe solution is poured into the above Cd(II)-Mea precursor solution and the mixture keeps stirring for 3 h at 95°C under N2 protection to obtain the Mea-CdTe quantum dots solution.

The synthesis of BTA@Mea-CdTe is based on the couple reaction of carboxylic acid and amine catalyzed by EDC/NHS [25]. 50 mL of 20 µM BTA solution was activated by EDC/NHS at room temperature for 20 min. Then 2 mL Mea-CdTe quantum dots solution was added and stirred at room temperature for 40 min. The QDs were collected by centrifugation at 10000 r min−1 for 5 min washing with ethanol and dried in vacuum at 40°C for 10 h. Then the prepared QDs were dispersed in ultrapure water for use.

The Hg2+ fluorescence titration spectra was detected by adding different volumes of 1.0×10−4 M Hg2+ ions to 4 mL 1×10−5 M diluted BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescent sensor. The Hg2+ ion concentration range is 0.2–1.2 µM.

The TEM image of the Mea-CdTe quantum dots is shown in Fig. 1 a. The synthesized Mea-CdTe quantum dots are spherical particles with a size of 3.35 nm and are uniformly dispersed. The higher resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) lattice fringe image of Mea-CdTe quantum dots is shown in Fig. 1b. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.373 nm could be indexed as the (111) characteristic crystal plane of cubic phase CdTe quantum dots (PDF No. 75-2086). It is similar to the reported result which confirms that Mea-CdTe quantum dots were successfully synthesized [27]. TEM and HRTEM images of BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor are shown in Fig. 1c and 1d. Compared with Fig. 1a, we can find that the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor has a more obvious aggregation phenomenon, which may be caused by the combination of the amino group on the Mea-CdTe quantum dot and the carboxyl group on the BTA. Compared with Fig. 1b, the lattice fringe spacing is 0.371 nm, indicating that the (311) characteristic crystal plane of the quantum dot has basically not changed. The above results indicate that the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor was successfully synthesized.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of Mea-CdTe quantum dots and mercaptamine hydrochloride are shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that the characteristic absorption band of thiol group at 2520 cm−1 in mercaptamine disappeared in Mea-CdTe quantum dots sample [28], which indicates that the mercaptamine sulfhydryl (S–H) group was broken during the reaction and S atom successfully bonded to the surface of CdTe quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of BTA@Mea-CdTe
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the infrared spectra of BTA, Mea-CdTe quantum dots and BTA@Mea-CdTe. It can be seen that the vibration peak of –C=O at 1685 cm−1 of BTA disappeared and a new absorption peak at 1600 cm−1 of amide bond (–CO–NH–) in BTA@Mea-CdTe sample appeared. It indicated that the carboxyl group of phencoumarin-3-carboxylic acid successfully reacted with the amino group of Mea-CdTe quantum dots to form an amide bond. The results confirm that the grafting of phencoumarin-3-carboxylic acid to the surface of Mea-CdTe quantum dots accomplished from amidation reaction. When we look closely, we can find that the 1750 cm−1 and 1685 cm−1 infrared absorption of BTA is associated with –C=O– stretching vibration peak [29], 1574 cm−1 is –CH=C– stretching vibration peak, 1402 cm−1 and 796 cm−1 are respectively υ C=C the skeleton vibration of the aromatic ring and the out-of-plane bending vibration peak of =C–H on the benzene ring. When BTA reacted with Mea-CdTe to generated BTA@Mea-CdTe, the stretching vibration peak of –CH=C– was overlapped with the peak of 1600 cm−1 (–CO–NH–), to form a broader absorption peak, and the peaks on the benzene ring still existed at 1356 cm−1 and 758 cm−1, respectively.

The UV-visible absorption spectrum of the BTA, bare Mea-CdTe and the constructed BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor is depicted in Fig. 4 a. From Fig. 4a, it can be found that BTA has two distinct characteristic absorption peaks at 227 nm and 356 nm, and Mea-CdTe quantum dots has a broad absorption peak at 545 nm. It can be seen that there are two distinct absorption bands at 340 nm and 550 nm of BTA@Mea-CdTe. The characteristic absorption peak at 340 nm is the absorption band of BTA, and the broad absorption peak at 550 nm is the absorption band of Mea-CdTe quantum dots. Compared to the BTA and bare Mea-CdTe, it is found that the two absorption peaks of the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor have a slight blue shift (from 356 nm to 340 nm) and red shift (from 545 nm to 550 nm), respectively. The fluorescence emission spectrum of the BTA, bare Mea-CdTe and the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor is shown in Fig. 4b. It can be found that BTA has a maximum fluorescence emission peak at 437 nm, and bare Mea-CdTe quantum dots also has a maximum fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm. The BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor has two fluorescence emission peaks at 436 nm and 575 nm. The fluorescence emission peak at 436 nm is the characteristic emission of BTA, and the fluorescence emission peak at 575 nm is the characteristic fluorescence emission of Mea-CdTe quantum dots. It is found that the two emission peaks of the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor also have slight shifts which agree with the UV-visible absorption spectrum. For the UV-vis absorption and fluorescence emission results, the two parts have little influence on each other. As described above, the constructed BTA@Mea-CdTe quantum dots could act as ratio fluorescence sensor for ion detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2 + ions by BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the selectivity of the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor for ion detection, the fluorescence response to some metal ions was tested, such as Mg2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+. The solution of the prepared BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor was diluted to 1×10−5 M. The above-mentioned different metal ions were separately added to the BTA@Mea-CdTe quantum dot solution, and the eventual concentration of metal ions is 1×10−5 M. The results were shown in Fig. 5a and 5b. It can be seen that the two fluorescence emission peaks of BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor have different responses. The addition of metal ions causes some changes in the fluorescence intensity of emission peak at 436 nm, but the effect is negligible. Although addition of other metal ions can slightly quench the emission fluorescence intensity at 575 nm, such as Ag+, Cu2+, and Ni2+, the fluorescence intensity of emission peak at 575 nm can be completely quenched only after adding Hg2+. The experimental results show that the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor has a selective detection of Hg2+ ions, and the fluorescence emission peak of BTA at 436 nm can be selected as the internal reference signal. Therefore, the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor has a potential application in the detection of Hg2+ ions in practical samples.

The competition experiment was also carried out by adding 10.0 µM Hg2+ to BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor solution in the presence of miscellaneous cations including Mg2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ (10.0 µM, respectively). The results were depicted in Fig. 6. The addition of other metal ions (except Hg2+ ions) has no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity at 575 nm of the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor. After adding equal concentration Hg2+ ions in the coexistence solution of the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor and other metal ions, the fluorescence intensity of the Mea-CdTe QDs is effectively quenched. It shows that the synthesized BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor has good selectivity and anti-ion interference for Hg2+ ion detection.

The fluorescence titration spectra of Mea-CdTe quantum dots with different concentrations of Hg2+ was shown in Fig. 7 a. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of CdTe quantum dots decreased with the Hg2+ concentration increased from 0 to 2 µM. This indicated that the presence of Hg2+ ions would quench the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots. Fig. 7b is a linear relationship diagram between the fluorescence quenching value (F 0−F) and the concentration of Hg2+ [30]. The linear correlation coefficient R 2=0.9596, the regression equation is Y=544.8X+327.6, Y is the quenching value of the quantum dot and X is the added Hg2+ concentration. The calculated detection limit is 0.021 µM according to the equation of LOD (limit of detection)=3σ/k (σ is the standard deviation of 10 measurements, k is the slope of linear range).

The fluorescence emission spectra of the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ ions were presented in Fig. 7c. With the increase of Hg2+ ion concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 575 nm of the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor is effectively quenched, where, the fluorescence intensity at 436 nm has very little change. So, the good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio of I 575/I 436 and Hg2+ ions in the range of 0.4–1.2 µM can be utilized to quantitatively analyze the content of mercury ion (Fig. 7d). The linear equation is expressed as I 575/I 436= 1.05−0.54C Hg 2+ (R2=0.9781). The detection limit of Hg2+ ions is 0.03 µM calculated by 3σ/k [31]. Comparing Mea-CdTe quantum dots with BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor, it is found that both sensors have low detection limits of the same order of magnitude for Hg2+ determination. Comparison with other reported literatures about Hg2+ detection, we will find that it is lower than the detection limit for Hg2+ in the currently reported literature. As shown in Table 1.

Interestingly, the BTA@Mea-CdTe ratio sensor makes it easier to visually detect mercury ion concentration from color changes. Mea-CdTe quantum dots can specifically recognize Hg2+ ions from fluorescence quenching, as shown on the left half of Fig. 8 . The BTA@Mea-CdTe quantum dots sensor recognizes Hg2+ of different concentrations as shown on the right half of Fig. 8. And we found that after adding Hg2+ to Mea-CdTe quantum dots and BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor, the fluorescence peaks are red-shifted, as shown in Fig. 7a and 7c. It may be that Hg2+ ions are combined with the amino groups on the surface of the quantum dots. Therefore, with the continuous addition of Hg2+ ions, the fluorescence emission of BTA is basically unchanged, while the fluorescence emission of Mea-CdTe quantum dots is quenched. Under the 340 nm irradiation of ultraviolet lamp, it is obvious that the fluorescent color of the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor changes from yellow to lavender. The phenomenon is more obvious than that of pure Mea-CdTe quantum dot. So BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor will have a more perfect visualization effect for Hg2+ ion recognition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response mechanism of BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor to Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the interaction mechanism between Hg2+ ions and the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor, FTIR spectroscopy and XPS characterization was performed with the sample after adding Hg2+ ions to the BTA@Mea-CdTe sensor. Compared with the infrared spectrum of the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor with/without Hg2+ ions, it can be seen that the infrared vibration band of the amino group at 1635 cm−1 on the surface of the quantum dots is significantly weakened (Fig. 9 a). Compared with the XPS of the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor with/without Hg2+ ions, it can be seen that the binding energy of N1s is slightly shifted (Fig. 9b). These indicate that Hg2+ ions might interact with the amino groups on the surface of Mea-CdTe quantum dots, quenching the fluorescence of Mea-CdTe quantum dots. This mechanism is similar to that of Chen et al. reported [41]. The electron transfer from Mea-CdTe quantum dots to Hg2+ bonding with the amino group leads to fluorescence quenching effect.

In order to explore the practical application of new sensor, the determination of Hg2+ in the actual water sample was performed. The spiked Hg2+ concentration was 0.6 µM, 0.8 µM, and 1.0 µM in tap water or lake water solution, respectively. Then, the fluorescence analysis was performed using the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescent sensor. According to the regression equation, the concentration of Hg2+ in the actual sample results are shown in Table 2 . It shows that the method has good accuracy, the BTA@Mea-CdTe fluorescence sensor can be used as the concentration detection of Hg2+ in the actual water sample.

A new BTA@Mea-CdTe ratio fluorescence sensor was constructed by the amidation reaction of BTA and Mea-CdTe quantum dots to determine Hg2+ ions in aqueous solution. Hg2+ ions bind to the amino groups on the surface of Mea-CdTe quantum dots to quench the fluorescence of Mea-CdTe quantum dots, but have little effect on the fluorescence emission of grafted BTA. Under the irradiation of 340 nm ultraviolet, BTA@Mea-CdTe ratio fluorescence sensor has the obvious color change with Hg2+ concentration increase. This phenomenon is more intuitive than the Hg2+ ion recognition of Mea-CdTe quantum dot. Therefore, the constructed BTA@Mea-CdTe ratio fluorescence sensor can be used for visual detection applications with obvious color change.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21404001, 51472001), the Anhui Province University Natural Science Research Project (KJ2020A0014), and the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1808085ME155).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2022.100070.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Perinatal lung development following maternal exposure to methylmercuric chloride

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.M. Das, J.E. Scott. Perinatal lung development following maternal exposure to methylmercuric chloride. Pediatr Pulmonol, 1994, 17(1): 11-21.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile Hg2+-related quenching photoluminescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.M. Du, B.B. Shang, X.R. Zhang, F. Feng, S.H. Zhang, B.P. Qi. A facile Hg2+-related quenching photoluminescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. Bull Korean Chem Soc, 2020, 41(9): 948-953.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient polymer pendant approach toward high stable organic fluorophore for sensing ultratrace Hg2+ with improved biological compatibility and cell permeability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Wei, Z.Q. Yan, J.C. Tian, G. Zhao, S.Y. Guang, H.Y. Xu. Efficient polymer pendant approach toward high stable organic fluorophore for sensing ultratrace Hg2+ with improved biological compatibility and cell permeability. Anal Chem, 2020, 92(4): 3293-3301.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A peroxidase-mimicking nanosensor with Hg2+-triggered enzymatic activity of cysteine-decorated ferromagnetic particles for ultrasensitive Hg2+ detection in environmental and biological fluids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.H. Niu, Y.F. He, X. Li, H.L. Zhao, J.M. Pan, F.X. Qiu, M.B. Lan. A peroxidase-mimicking nanosensor with Hg2+-triggered enzymatic activity of cysteine-decorated ferromagnetic particles for ultrasensitive Hg2+ detection in environmental and biological fluids. Sensors Actuator B-Chem, 2019, 281: 445-452.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mercury in environmental samples: speciation, artifacts and validation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Leermakers, W. Baeyens, P. Quevauviller, M. Horvat. Mercury in environmental samples: speciation, artifacts and validation. Trac-Trend Anal Chem, 2005, 24(5): 383-393.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conformation-dependent exonuclease III activity mediated by metal ions reshuffling on thymine-Rich DNA duplexes for an ultrasensitive electrochemical method for Hg2+ detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Xuan, X. Luo, I.M. Hsing. Conformation-dependent exonuclease III activity mediated by metal ions reshuffling on thymine-Rich DNA duplexes for an ultrasensitive electrochemical method for Hg2+ detection. Anal Chem, 2013, 85(9): 4586-4593.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new room temperature ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-trimethylsilylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate as a solvent for extraction and preconcentration of mercury with determination by cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry

A new room temperature ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-trimethylsilylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate as a solvent for extraction and preconcentration of mercury with determination by cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry. Talanta, 2007, 71(1): 68-72.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Online mercury speciation through liquid chromatography with particle beam/electron ionization mass spectrometry detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.V.B. Krishna, J. Castro, T.M. Brewer, R.K. Marcus. Online mercury speciation through liquid chromatography with particle beam/electron ionization mass spectrometry detection. J Anal At Spectrom, 2007, 22(3): 283-291.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Utilizing host-guest interaction enables the simultaneous enhancement of the quantum yield and Stokes shift in organosilane-functionalized, nitrogen-containing carbon dots for laminated luminescent solar concentrators

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.Y. Huang, M.J. Talite, K.B. Cai, R.J. Soebroto, S.H. Chang, W.R. Liu, W.C. Chou, C.T. Yuan. Utilizing host-guest interaction enables the simultaneous enhancement of the quantum yield and Stokes shift in organosilane-functionalized, nitrogen-containing carbon dots for laminated luminescent solar concentrators. Nanoscale, 2020, 12(46): 23537-23545.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioconjugated quantum dots for in vivo molecular and cellular imaging
